title: India — Physical Features & Climate
highYieldFacts: India lies in the Northern and Eastern hemispheres.
Latitudinal extent: 8°4'N to 37°6'N; longitudinal extent: 68°7'E to 97°25'E.
Tropic of Cancer (23°30'N) divides India into tropical and sub-tropical parts.
Standard Meridian is 82°30'E, giving IST (UTC+5:30).
India’s peninsular location gives it a central position in the Indian Ocean.
The Himalayas are young fold mountains formed by Indo-Eurasian plate collision.
Himadri is the highest Himalayan range with permanent snow and glaciers.
Shivaliks are the outermost foothills made of unconsolidated sediments.
Purvanchal hills include Patkai, Naga and Lushai.
Northern Plains are formed by alluvium from Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra rivers.
Bhabar is a porous pebble belt; Terai is marshy with tall grasses.
Khadar is new alluvium; Bhangar is old alluvium.
Peninsular Plateau is the oldest landmass of India, part of Gondwana.
Central Highlands include Malwa, Bundelkhand and Chota Nagpur plateau.
Deccan Plateau is basaltic and formed by lava flows.
Western Ghats are higher and continuous; Eastern Ghats are lower and broken.
Nilgiri hills are the meeting point of Eastern and Western Ghats.
Western Coastal Plains are narrow; Eastern Coastal Plains are wider with deltas.
Andaman & Nicobar Islands are volcanic; Lakshadweep Islands are coral.
Himalayan rivers are perennial and form extensive flood plains and deltas.
Peninsular rivers are seasonal and mostly rain-fed.
Narmada and Tapti flow west through rift valleys to the Arabian Sea.
Godavari is the largest peninsular river.
India has a monsoon-type climate with seasonal reversal of winds.
South-west monsoon arrives in Kerala around 1 June.
Retreating monsoon brings rain to Tamil Nadu in October-November.
Orographic rainfall occurs on windward slopes; leeward sides are rain-shadow.
Mawsynram/Cherrapunji receive some of the highest rainfall globally.
Vegetation depends on rainfall, temperature, soil and altitude.
Tropical deciduous forests are the most widespread type in India.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** India stretches from 8°4'N to 37°6'N and 68°7'E to 97°25'E, centrally placed in the Indian Ocean.

**Must know**
• Latitudinal extent: 8°4'N (Kanyakumari) to 37°6'N (Kashmir)
• Longitudinal extent: 68°7'E (Gujarat) to 97°25'E (Arunachal Pradesh)
• Tropic of Cancer (23°30'N) bisects India into tropical and sub-tropical halves
• Standard Meridian: 82°30'E, basis of IST (UTC+5:30)
• Central location gives India sea routes to West Asia, Africa and Europe

India is located in the Northern and Eastern hemispheres. Its peninsular shape projects into the Indian Ocean, giving it a strategic position on ancient and modern sea routes. The Tropic of Cancer passes through the middle of the country, creating contrasts in climate and day length between north and south. Longitudinally, India spans almost 30 degrees; hence a standard meridian (82°30'E) was chosen to maintain a single time zone (IST).
notes.explanation:
ICSE frequently tests latitude-longitude facts, the role of the Tropic of Cancer, and the IST calculation. Link location to monsoon influence and maritime trade.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-02 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Ask students to locate Kanyakumari, Indira Point and the Standard Meridian on an outline map.
notes.examTip: Write lat/long ranges exactly as 8°4'N–37°6'N and 68°7'E–97°25'E.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** The Himalayas are young fold mountains formed by Indo-Eurasian plate collision and divided into Himadri, Himachal and Shivalik.

**Must know**
• Himadri (Greater Himalaya): highest, snow-bound, source of glaciers
• Himachal (Lesser Himalaya): ranges like Pir Panjal and Dhauladhar
• Shivalik (Outer Himalaya): low foothills with unconsolidated sediments
• Purvanchal hills extend eastwards (Patkai, Naga, Lushai)
• Act as a climatic barrier and source of perennial rivers

The Himalayas were formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates and are among the youngest fold mountains. They consist of three parallel ranges: Himadri (highest and snow-covered), Himachal (middle ranges with valleys and hill stations) and the Shivaliks (outer foothills). Eastwards they bend into the Purvanchal hills. The Himalayas block cold continental winds and force monsoon winds to rise, causing heavy rainfall on the southern slopes.
notes.explanation:
Explain the threefold division and the climatic role. UPSC-style MCQs often test the names and characteristics of Himalayan ranges.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-01 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch7-03 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Use a cross-sectional diagram to show the three parallel ranges and their relative heights.
notes.examTip: Mention that the Himalayas are young fold mountains and the source of perennial rivers.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** The Northern Plains are formed by alluvium from the Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra systems, creating India's most fertile belt.

**Must know**
• Extends from Punjab to Assam; broad, flat and densely populated
• Formed by alluvial deposits brought by Himalayan rivers
• Regional divisions: Punjab Plains, Ganga Plains, Brahmaputra Plains
• Bhabar (pebbly belt) and Terai (marshy belt) along the foothills
• Khadar (new alluvium) and Bhangar (old alluvium)

The Northern Plains lie south of the Himalayas and stretch from Punjab in the west to Assam in the east. They are created by thick deposits of alluvium laid by the Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra river systems. The plains are agriculturally productive and densely populated. Along the foothills are distinct belts: the porous Bhabar, the marshy Terai, and further south the fertile alluvial plains with Khadar and Bhangar soils.
notes.explanation:
Key terms like Bhabar, Terai, Khadar and Bhangar are high-yield. Link fertility to intensive agriculture and dense population.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-02 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch7-04 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Use a profile diagram from Shivalik foothills to the alluvial plains to show Bhabar and Terai.
notes.examTip: Define Khadar vs Bhangar using 'new' vs 'old' alluvium.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** The Peninsular Plateau is an ancient, stable block of igneous and metamorphic rocks, divided into Central Highlands and Deccan Plateau.

**Must know**
• Oldest landmass of India; part of Gondwana
• Central Highlands include Malwa, Bundelkhand, Chota Nagpur
• Deccan Plateau south of Narmada; basaltic lava flows
• Western Ghats are higher and continuous; Eastern Ghats are lower and discontinuous
• Nilgiri hills form the meeting point of both Ghats

The Peninsular Plateau is one of the oldest landmasses, formed from hard crystalline rocks. It is relatively stable, with broad plateaus and residual hills. The plateau is divided into the Central Highlands in the north and the Deccan Plateau in the south. The Western Ghats form a steep escarpment along the western coast, while the Eastern Ghats are lower and broken, merging into the plateau. The Nilgiri hills link the two.
notes.explanation:
Students should contrast the Western and Eastern Ghats and connect the plateau to mineral resources and black soil.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-03 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch7-05 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Mark the plateau divisions and Ghats on an outline map for map practice.
notes.examTip: State that the plateau is composed of hard igneous and metamorphic rocks.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** India has narrow western and broader eastern coastal plains, along with the Andaman & Nicobar and Lakshadweep Islands.

**Must know**
• Western Coastal Plains: narrow, Konkan–Kannad–Malabar sections
• Eastern Coastal Plains: wider, includes the Northern Circars and Coromandel
• Eastern coast has large deltas (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri)
• Andaman & Nicobar Islands in Bay of Bengal; volcanic origin
• Lakshadweep Islands in Arabian Sea; coral origin

The Western Coastal Plains are narrow due to the proximity of the Western Ghats, while the Eastern Coastal Plains are wider and more depositional, with large river deltas. The Andaman & Nicobar Islands are a chain of volcanic islands in the Bay of Bengal, while Lakshadweep comprises coral islands in the Arabian Sea. India’s coastal plains support ports, fisheries and major urban centres.
notes.explanation:
Differentiate between western and eastern coasts in terms of width, rivers and deltas. Islands are often asked separately.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-04 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch7-06 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Show the 10° Channel separating Andaman and Nicobar and the Gulf of Mannar coral area.
notes.examTip: Remember: Lakshadweep = coral; Andaman & Nicobar = volcanic.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** India’s rivers are broadly Himalayan (perennial) and Peninsular (seasonal), shaping plains, deltas and agriculture.

**Must know**
• Himalayan rivers: Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra; snow-fed and perennial
• Peninsular rivers: Godavari, Krishna, Mahanadi (east-flowing) and Narmada, Tapti (west-flowing)
• Himalayan rivers form extensive flood plains and deltaic regions
• Peninsular rivers have shorter courses and seasonal flow
• Rivers are vital for irrigation, hydropower and transport

Himalayan rivers are long, snow-fed and carry heavy loads of silt, forming broad flood plains and deltas. The Indus system includes Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas and Sutlej; the Ganga system includes Yamuna, Ghaghara, Gandak and Kosi. The Brahmaputra flows as Tsangpo in Tibet and Jamuna in Bangladesh. Peninsular rivers are rain-fed and flow over hard rock beds, forming waterfalls and narrow valleys.
notes.explanation:
Emphasise perennial vs seasonal and east-flowing vs west-flowing differences; map questions are common.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-05 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch7-07 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Ask students to label the five tributaries of Indus on a map.
notes.examTip: Mention that Narmada and Tapti flow west through rift valleys.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** India has a monsoon-type climate with seasonal reversal of winds and four main seasons.

**Must know**
• Seasons: cold weather, hot weather, south-west monsoon, retreating monsoon
• Monsoon winds reverse direction seasonally due to land-sea temperature contrast
• Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal branches bring rain from June to September
• Orographic rainfall on windward slopes; rain-shadow on leeward side
• Retreating monsoon causes rain in Tamil Nadu (Oct-Nov)

India’s climate is dominated by the monsoon, defined by seasonal reversal of winds. During summer, low pressure over the north attracts moist winds from the oceans, bringing heavy rainfall. The Arabian Sea branch strikes the Western Ghats first, while the Bay of Bengal branch moves towards the northeast and then westwards along the Himalayas. In winter, dry winds blow from land to sea. The retreating monsoon causes rainfall on the Coromandel Coast.
notes.explanation:
Connect monsoon with relief, rainfall distribution and agriculture. UPSC-style questions often test causes of rainfall variability.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-06 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch7-08 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Use a monsoon wind diagram and highlight onset dates and branches.
notes.examTip: Write 'seasonal reversal of winds' when defining monsoon.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** India’s vegetation ranges from tropical evergreen to alpine forests, influenced by rainfall, altitude and soil.

**Must know**
• Tropical evergreen: heavy rainfall areas of Western Ghats and NE India
• Tropical deciduous: most widespread, found in central and northern India
• Thorn and scrub: arid and semi-arid regions of Rajasthan and Gujarat
• Mangroves: tidal deltas like Sundarbans
• Wildlife protection through national parks, sanctuaries and Project Tiger

Natural vegetation varies with climate and relief. Dense evergreen forests occur in high rainfall areas; deciduous forests dominate the peninsular and northern plains; thorn forests cover arid regions. Mangroves thrive in deltaic and tidal environments. India protects its biodiversity through national parks, sanctuaries and biosphere reserves. Project Tiger (1973) and other conservation efforts focus on protecting endangered species.
notes.explanation:
Link vegetation to rainfall and altitude. Conservation programmes are a key exam focus.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch7-07 — previous topic
notes.teacherTip: Compare deciduous vs evergreen with a quick rainfall chart.
notes.examTip: Mention Sundarbans as the largest mangrove forest in India.

questions.question: India's latitudinal extent is approximately:
questions.answer: India stretches from about 8°4'N to 37°6'N, from Kanyakumari to Kashmir.
questions.examTip: Exact latitude range is a high-yield fact.

questions.question: India's longitudinal extent is:
questions.answer: The east-west extent is roughly 68°7'E to 97°25'E.
questions.examTip: This explains the large time lag between east and west.

questions.question: The Tropic of Cancer passes through India at:
questions.answer: The Tropic of Cancer is at 23°30'N and divides India into tropical and sub-tropical parts.

questions.question: How many Indian states does the Tropic of Cancer pass through?
questions.answer: The Tropic of Cancer passes through 8 states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, MP, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, WB, Tripura, Mizoram.
questions.examTip: ICSE often asks the number of states.

questions.question: The Standard Meridian of India is:
questions.answer: India uses 82°30'E as its Standard Meridian to keep a single time zone.

questions.question: Indian Standard Time is ahead of GMT by:
questions.answer: IST is GMT + 5 hours 30 minutes because 82°30'E is 5.5 hours ahead of 0°.

questions.question: The Andaman & Nicobar Islands lie in the:
questions.answer: Andaman & Nicobar Islands are in the Bay of Bengal.

questions.question: Lakshadweep Islands are of:
questions.answer: Lakshadweep consists of coral islands in the Arabian Sea.
questions.examTip: Coral vs volcanic is a standard comparison.

questions.question: The Himalayas were formed due to:
questions.answer: The Himalayas are young fold mountains formed by collision of Indian and Eurasian plates.

questions.question: The highest and northernmost Himalayan range is:
questions.answer: Himadri (Greater Himalaya) is the highest range with permanent snow.

questions.question: The Shivalik range is characterized by:
questions.answer: Shivaliks are the outermost foothills made of loose, unconsolidated sediments.

questions.question: Which hills form the eastern extension of the Himalayas?
questions.answer: The Himalayas curve eastwards into the Purvanchal hills (Patkai, Naga, Lushai).

questions.question: The Northern Plains were formed by:
questions.answer: These plains are built by alluvial deposits of three major river systems.

questions.question: The porous pebble belt along the foothills is called:
questions.answer: Bhabar is the pebbly, porous belt along the Shivalik foothills.

questions.question: The marshy, forested belt south of Bhabar is:
questions.answer: Terai is marshy, with tall grasses and dense forests.

questions.question: Old alluvium in the Northern Plains is known as:
questions.answer: Bhangar is the older alluvium, often with kankar nodules.

questions.question: The Peninsular Plateau is mainly composed of:
questions.answer: The plateau is a stable block of ancient crystalline rocks.

questions.question: The Central Highlands include:
questions.answer: Central Highlands include Malwa, Bundelkhand and Chota Nagpur regions.

questions.question: The Deccan Plateau lies to the south of the:
questions.answer: The Deccan Plateau lies south of the Narmada river.

questions.question: The Western Ghats are:
questions.answer: Western Ghats are higher and more continuous than Eastern Ghats.

questions.question: The highest peak in the Western Ghats is:
questions.answer: Anai Mudi is the highest peak of the Western Ghats.

questions.question: The Eastern Ghats are best described as:
questions.answer: Eastern Ghats are broken and discontinuous due to river erosion.

questions.question: The Nilgiri Hills are important because they:
options: Join the Eastern and Western Ghats
questions.answer: Nilgiri Hills are the meeting point of the Eastern and Western Ghats.

questions.question: Which coastal plain is wider and has large deltas?
questions.answer: The Eastern Coastal Plains are broader and have large deltas of east-flowing rivers.

questions.question: The Coromandel Coast lies on the:
questions.answer: Coromandel Coast is the southeastern part of the Eastern Coastal Plains.

questions.question: The largest mangrove forest in India is:
questions.answer: The Sundarbans, in the Ganga-Brahmaputra delta, are the largest mangroves in India.

questions.question: Which statement about Andaman & Nicobar Islands is correct?
options: They are part of the Deccan Plateau
questions.answer: Andaman & Nicobar Islands are volcanic in origin and located in the Bay of Bengal.

questions.question: The 10° Channel separates:
questions.answer: The 10° Channel separates Andaman and Nicobar Islands.

questions.question: Himalayan rivers are termed perennial because they:
options: Are fed by glaciers and rainfall
questions.answer: Himalayan rivers receive snowmelt and rainfall, so they flow year-round.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a tributary of the Indus?
questions.answer: Gandak is a tributary of the Ganga, not of the Indus.

questions.question: The Brahmaputra is known as the Tsangpo in:
questions.answer: In Tibet, the Brahmaputra is called Tsangpo.

questions.question: Which two peninsular rivers flow westwards to the Arabian Sea?
questions.answer: Narmada and Tapti flow west through rift valleys to the Arabian Sea.

questions.question: The largest peninsular river is:
questions.answer: Godavari is the largest peninsular river, often called the Dakshin Ganga.

questions.question: Which river forms a large delta in Odisha?
questions.answer: The Mahanadi forms a prominent delta along the Odisha coast.

questions.question: India has a monsoon-type climate because of:
questions.answer: Monsoon climate is defined by seasonal reversal of winds driven by land-sea contrast.

questions.question: The south-west monsoon generally arrives in Kerala around:
questions.answer: The onset date is around 1 June in Kerala.

questions.question: The monsoon branch that brings rain to the Western Ghats first is:
questions.answer: The Arabian Sea branch hits the Western Ghats first, causing orographic rainfall.

questions.question: Retreating monsoon rains are important for:
questions.answer: Tamil Nadu receives significant rainfall from the retreating monsoon in Oct-Nov.

questions.question: Orographic rainfall occurs when:
options: Moist winds are forced to rise over mountains
Winds blow from land to sea
questions.answer: Mountains force moist winds to rise, cool and condense, causing heavy rain on windward slopes.

questions.question: The rain-shadow region of the Western Ghats lies largely in:
questions.answer: Leeward side of Western Ghats (Deccan Plateau) receives less rainfall.

questions.question: The highest rainfall in India is recorded at:
questions.answer: Mawsynram and Cherrapunji in Meghalaya receive some of the highest rainfall in the world.

questions.question: The Thar Desert receives low rainfall mainly because:
options: Aravalli range runs parallel to monsoon winds
questions.answer: The Aravallis run parallel to SW monsoon winds, offering little orographic lift.

questions.question: Tropical evergreen forests are found in:
questions.answer: Evergreen forests grow in high rainfall areas like Western Ghats and NE India.

questions.question: The most widespread natural vegetation in India is:
questions.answer: Tropical deciduous forests are the most widespread type in India.

questions.question: Thorn forests are typical of:
questions.answer: Thorn forests occur in arid and semi-arid areas like Rajasthan and Gujarat.

questions.question: Mangroves are best developed in:
questions.answer: Mangroves thrive in tidal deltas; the Sundarbans is the largest.

questions.question: Montane forests are associated with:
questions.answer: Montane forests occur with altitude in the Himalayas and other highlands.

questions.question: Project Tiger in India began in:
questions.answer: Project Tiger was launched in 1973 to protect tigers and habitats.

questions.question: India's peninsular location gives it:
options: Access to major sea routes of the Indian Ocean
questions.answer: India’s central position in the Indian Ocean facilitates maritime trade routes.

questions.question: The Northern Plains are densely populated mainly because:
questions.answer: Fertile alluvial soils and flat land support agriculture and dense settlement.

questions.question: A doab refers to:
questions.answer: Doab is the land between two rivers, common in the Ganga-Yamuna region.

questions.question: Which of the following is a west-flowing river?
questions.answer: Narmada flows westward into the Arabian Sea through a rift valley.

questions.question: The western coastal plain is divided into:
questions.answer: Konkan, Kannad and Malabar are divisions of the western coast.

questions.question: The eastern coastal plain includes:
questions.answer: The Coromandel Coast is part of the eastern coastal plains.

questions.question: Black soil is associated mainly with:
questions.answer: Black soil (regur) is formed from basaltic lava of the Deccan Plateau.

questions.question: India's climate is described as monsoon because:
options: It has a marked seasonal reversal of winds
questions.answer: Seasonal reversal of winds and distinct seasons define monsoon climate.

questions.question: Which season is characterized by hot, dry winds called loo?
questions.answer: Loo winds blow in the hot weather season (April-June) in the northern plains.

questions.question: Pre-monsoon showers in Kerala and Karnataka are called:
questions.answer: Mango showers are pre-monsoon showers that help ripen mangoes.

questions.question: Cyclones in the Bay of Bengal are common during:
questions.answer: Bay of Bengal cyclones are frequent during Oct-Nov in the retreating monsoon season.

questions.question: Vegetation distribution in India depends mainly on:
questions.answer: Climate, soil and relief control vegetation types.

questions.question: Which is the highest peak in India (within Indian territory)?
questions.answer: Kanchenjunga (8,586 m) is the highest peak within Indian territory.

questions.question: The Brahmaputra enters India as:
questions.answer: It enters Arunachal Pradesh as the Dihang before becoming Brahmaputra.

questions.question: The Ganga-Yamuna Doab lies between:
questions.answer: The Ganga-Yamuna Doab is the land between the Yamuna and Ganga rivers.

questions.question: Which river is known as the 'Dakshin Ganga'?
questions.answer: Godavari is called the Dakshin Ganga because of its length and basin size.

questions.question: The Brahmaputra in Bangladesh is called:
questions.answer: In Bangladesh, the Brahmaputra is called the Jamuna.

questions.question: The Malabar Coast lies along which state group?
questions.answer: The Malabar Coast is along Kerala and Karnataka.

questions.question: The Northern Plains are also called:
questions.answer: They are formed by Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra river systems.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct pair?
questions.answer: Bhabar is the porous pebble belt; Khadar is new alluvium; Bhangar is old.

questions.question: Which pair of rivers is most associated with rift valleys?
questions.answer: Narmada and Tapti flow through rift valleys between Vindhya and Satpura ranges.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a rain-shadow region?
questions.answer: Deccan Plateau lies on the leeward side of the Western Ghats and is a rain-shadow region.

questions.question: The Tropic of Cancer divides India into tropical and sub-tropical regions.
questions.answer: It runs at 23°30'N across the middle of India.

questions.question: Himadri is the lowest Himalayan range.
questions.answer: Himadri is the highest range; Shivalik is the lowest.

questions.question: The Northern Plains are formed by the alluvium of the Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra.
questions.answer: These river systems deposit thick alluvium.

questions.question: The Western Ghats are lower and more discontinuous than the Eastern Ghats.
questions.answer: Western Ghats are higher and more continuous.

questions.question: Lakshadweep Islands are of coral origin.
questions.answer: Lakshadweep is made of coral reefs in the Arabian Sea.

questions.question: Himalayan rivers are seasonal and dry up in summer.
questions.answer: They are perennial due to snowmelt and rainfall.

questions.question: Narmada and Tapti flow eastwards into the Bay of Bengal.
questions.answer: They flow westwards into the Arabian Sea.

questions.question: India experiences a monsoon climate with seasonal reversal of winds.
questions.answer: This is the defining feature of monsoon climate.

questions.question: Tropical deciduous forests are the most widespread in India.
questions.answer: They cover large parts of the peninsular and northern plains.

questions.question: Mangroves grow in arid regions with very low rainfall.
questions.answer: Mangroves grow in tidal, saline delta regions.

questions.question: India's Standard Meridian is _____ longitude.
questions.answer: 82°30'E determines IST for the whole country.

questions.question: The highest Himalayan range is called the _____.
questions.answer: Himadri is the Greater Himalaya.

questions.question: The marshy belt south of Bhabar is called the _____.
questions.answer: Terai is swampy and densely forested.

questions.question: The largest peninsular river is the _____.
questions.answer: Godavari is called the Dakshin Ganga.

questions.question: The largest mangrove forest in India is the _____.
questions.answer: Sundarbans lies in the Ganga-Brahmaputra delta.

questions.question: Match range with feature:
questions.answer: Himadri-highest; Himachal-middle; Shivalik-foothills; Purvanchal-east

questions.question: Match plain term with description:
questions.answer: Bhabar-pebbly; Terai-marshy; Khadar-new; Bhangar-old

questions.question: Match coastal section with coast:
questions.answer: Konkan-west; Malabar-west; Coromandel-east; Circars-east

questions.question: Match island group with origin:
questions.answer: A&N-volcanic; Lakshadweep-coral; Sundarbans-mangrove; Mannar-coral

questions.question: Match river with feature:
questions.answer: Narmada-west; Godavari-largest; Brahmaputra-Tsangpo; Kaveri-delta

questions.question: Match monsoon term with meaning:
questions.answer: Monsoon-reversal; Orographic-windward; Rain-shadow-leeward; Retreating-TN

questions.question: Match vegetation with region:
questions.answer: Evergreen-Ghats/NE; Deciduous-central; Thorn-Rajasthan; Mangrove-Sundarbans

questions.question: Match feature with statement:
questions.answer: W Ghats-higher; E Ghats-lower; Nilgiri-meeting; Deccan-basaltic

questions.question: Match state with Tropic of Cancer:
questions.answer: Gujarat-yes; Rajasthan-yes; Tripura-yes; Kerala-no

questions.question: Match seasonal wind with effect:
questions.answer: SW-Jun-Sept; NE-Oct-Nov; Loo-hot; Winter-dry

questions.question: Explain why India’s location is considered strategic.
questions.answer: India’s peninsular location in the Indian Ocean places it on major sea routes linking West Asia, Africa and Europe with Southeast Asia. It also allows maritime trade, access to resources and influence over the Indian Ocean region.

questions.question: Differentiate between Himadri, Himachal and Shivalik ranges.
questions.answer: Himadri is the highest range with permanent snow and glaciers. Himachal (Lesser Himalaya) has lower ranges with valleys and hill stations. Shivalik is the outermost foothill range of unconsolidated sediments.

questions.question: What is the difference between Bhangar and Khadar?
questions.answer: Bhangar is the older alluvium with kankar nodules and slightly elevated terraces. Khadar is newer, finer alluvium deposited annually by floods, making it more fertile.

questions.question: Why is the western coastal plain narrow while the eastern coastal plain is wide?
questions.answer: The Western Ghats lie close to the coast, leaving a narrow coastal strip. On the east, rivers flow longer distances and deposit extensive deltas, creating a wider plain.

questions.question: State any four factors influencing natural vegetation in India.
questions.answer: Rainfall, temperature, soil type and altitude are the main factors; human activity also alters natural vegetation.
